campaigns.title: Sanjivani- mother to 70 kids rescued from red-light areas, needs help to bring more to safety and care for them
campaigns.description: Renu grew up in the chawls of Pune’s red-light area in a dingy house, witnessing a flow of unknown men enter and leave their small bedroom day and night. Sometimes while looking at her mother hiding her fresh bruises with her pallu, Renu’s determination not to take up this path that her mother was pushed into only became stronger. She yearned for a way out of this dark world where she could live without fear, not struggle for three meals a day and get an education.Luckily for Renu (name changed), Swadhar IDWC-Pune took her in with her mother’s consent and put her in school. After years of hard work, she cleared all her grades and has now appeared for her Class 12 final exams.Swadhar IDWC led by Sanjivani Hingne, is on a mission to bring many more children to safety and your support can make it possible. Donate generously.Donate easily through UPI, all debit/credit cards and net banking. Charitable donations to this fundraising campaign are tax exempted under 80G of the Income Tax Act (India).A fight for the rights of childrenChildren like Renu, who are born to sex workers, cannot dream of a normal childhood. They are subjected to stress, trauma, and sometimes abuse by their mother’s clients. “What will the child get whose mother is a sex worker? There is no family; there is no schooling, no medical care, and nothing for the child. Children like them are easy prey. Our only concern is the child getting into mainstream society and not continuing in the mother’s profession,” says Sanjivani Hingne, secretary, Swadhar IDWC Pune.Swadhar IDWC- Pune weaves a safety net for children of sex workers. They strive to prevent second-generation prostitution of children living in red-light areas by providing a secure environment where they have access to shelter, nutrition, healthcare, education, and happiness. The organisation wants to provide a refuge for more children trapped in the sex-trade environment, but they cannot do it without your support.Project Mohor - a safe home away from red-light areasSwadhar’s fully functional 24x7 facility looks after the most vulnerable and neglected children of sex workers in Pune’s red-light area. These children often roam the streets till early morning while their mothers engage in prostitution. In view of providing a safe environment for them, Swadhar runs a kitchen, a pre-school, and an e-learning center.“Right from cleaning, bathing, feeding, medical care, seeing that the child is ready for school - all of it is taken care of here, which is impossible in a red-light area. So far, we have taken care of 200 children, most of them now pursuing higher education or have finished school with flying colours. Now, we need more funds because the number of children is increasing and we want to take in more children”.Unfortunately, this safe home for kids is at risk of shutting down. The NGO is racing against time to raise funds to build a new home for the rescued kids and your help can strengthen their efforts. Please donate as much as you can.Donate to this fundraiser on Give.do, India’s largest and most trusted online donation platform, to help minor victims of forced prostitution get back to school and reshape their lives.

campaigns.title: Help Gopalan of Akshaya Trust feed and care for rescued abandoned elderly parents
campaigns.description: “Parvathi Amma (name changed) was brought to Akshaya Trust by her son. He brought her here saying she was being taken to the hospital for treatment and he would be back in a while. It's been 8 years since that day. The phone number he gave to us was made up, even his address was false. Imagine the pain of the mother who was abandoned in such a heartless manner. She is not the only one. We have over 260 elderly parents here and most of them have faced abandonment, neglect or even abuse”, says G Gopalan, the founder of Akshaya Trust, the NGO taking care of abandoned seniors who have no one else in the world.Without Gopalan’s Akshaya Trust, countless elderly would still be on the streets or simply deprived of the love and care they deserve. The NGO takes care of every single need of its seniors from Food to medical care and housing. Your donation can enable Akshaya Trust to reach out to many more elderly with food and support. Contribute generously. Donate easily through UPI, all debit/credit cards and net banking. Charitable donations to this fundraising campaign are tax exempted under 80G of the Income Tax Act (India).G Gopalan, a well-to-do businessman preferred to serve the elderly than living a peaceful retired life. He says that he was too shocked to see the state of the elderly, treated shabbily by their own children.A lifeline for hundreds of neglected elderlyToday, Akshaya Trust offers a safe haven to over 260 elderly parents, shielding them from the harsh realities of street life. This organization attends to all their needs, providing comprehensive medical care and ensuring they recover from past traumas. The trust also organises joyful celebrations during festivals and special occasions, bringing happiness into their lives.To date, Akshaya Trust has served over 1,000 elderly parents most of whom were from extremely weak financial backgrounds and had no way to fend for themselves. Akshaya Trust: A silver lining for elderlyAkshaya Trust’s efforts are a lifeline for these seniors, but they rely heavily on public support to continue. You can be the hero these elderly parents deserve. Donate now to help care for them.Your contribution can transform the lives of many homeless seniors. Support Akshaya Trust’s mission today.Donate to this fundraiser on Give.do, India’s largest and most trusted online donation platform, to help elderly parents abandoned by their children get the love and care they deserve.

campaigns.title: For tribal children of Majuli Island, education is the only way out. Help Bipin ensure they go to school
campaigns.description: “The Hummingbird School is my only hope to succeed in life. I feel I am trying to swim out of this place (Majuli) and my school stands as a beacon on the other side of the river. If my school closes down, I feel I will drown in this river. Not just me but every child in villages around here and our families depend on this school”, says Roopjyoti, a bright, young student of Ayang Trust’s The Hummingbird School.Majuli Island, where The Hummingbird School is situated, faces severe floods and constant riverbank erosion, leaving most of its residents struggling to survive. For the children of this impoverished tribal community, education remains their only path to hope and dignity.Your support to Ayang Trust’s Hummingbird School can ensure that its students can have a future different from their parents who are struggling to get even two meals a day. Donate now.Donate easily through UPI, all debit/credit cards and net banking. Charitable donations to this fundraising campaign are tax exempted under 80G of the Income Tax Act (India).From IIT Kharagpur to Majuli“When I first visited the remote villages of Majuli I was shocked. The extent of the struggles here was beyond my imagination. These villages had no connectivity to the outside world, and very limited access to healthcare and quality education”, says Bipin Dhane, founder of the school. Bipin, who grew up in the small town of Satara in Maharashtra, faced countless financial hardships. Despite his struggles, Bipin secured admission to the prestigious Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, and then went on to take up a job in Singapore. Owing to his childhood experiences Bipin was always inclined towards helping the less fortunate, and he believed in the transformative power of education. It was in 2016 that Bipin left his lucrative career abroad and moved to Majuli, to provide the children from this inaccessible river island education they were deprived of.“Whatever little money I had saved, I gave it to my parents. I had no money to start the school. It was then that villagers came together to build bamboo huts. Each family donated bamboo, wood, and whatever little they could. It was the parents of our students who erected huts for the school to start”, shares Bipin while talking about the inception of The Hummingbird School in 2017.Help Bipin save underprivileged children of Majuli Island from generational poverty and give them a chance to succeed. Donate now.Donate to save future generations“People of the island value education greatly because they know that their children have no future without it. Parents have vested their faith in The Humming Bird School because they don't want their children to suffer the way they have been”, says Bipin. Bipin says, “Today we have over 300 students in our school and about 90 children live in our hostel. Many of these children are orphaned or come from single-parent households, struggling each day without adequate care or resources.. I urge each one of you to come forward and donate to us so we can help hundreds of children escape from the cycle of poverty and build lives of dignity, hope, and opportunity.”Donate to this fundraiser by Give, India’s largest and most trusted online donation platform, to help children of Majuli Island get an education and break the cycle of poverty.

campaigns.title: Help Leena in saving girls from being lost forever in red-light areas
campaigns.description: "Hundreds of young girls are lost forever in the dark lanes of red-light areas," says Leena Kejriwal, the founder of Missing Link Trust. These girls endure unimaginable abuse and exploitation, being lured or trafficked into an industry that breaks them physically and emotionally. Missing Link Trust is on a mission to prevent the tragedy before it happens, and also rehabilitate girls and women who are rescued from forced sex work.Leena’s journey began with her powerful street art project in Kolkata, where she painted haunting silhouettes of missing girls to raise awareness against sex trafficking. Now, she leads a larger fight — one that focuses on prevention and rehabilitation. But this mission needs your support.You can save countless girl children from going missing forever. Donate now.Donate easily through UPI, all debit/credit cards and net banking. Charitable donations to this fundraising campaign are tax exempted under 80G of the Income Tax Act (India).Preventing Trafficking Before It Begins“When girls come into a red-light area, the first thing is that they are broken, physically and emotionally,” Leena says. “It’s like they will be caught, hands and legs tied, and all heinous crimes committed on them.” These are the brutal realities that Missing Link Trust fights against every day.Many of these girls are from impoverished regions like the Sundarbans, and traffickers prey on their vulnerability. “It’s shocking that they sell themselves for as low as a hundred rupees,” Leena adds. "They ready her through violent acts, removing all threads of shame from her."But why wait until a girl is trafficked to save her? Missing Link Trust believes in stopping trafficking at its root — through “prevention, prevention, prevention” as Leena puts it. The goal is simple: protect girls from ever being lost to this darkness.A Safe World for Children“Our mission is the prevention of abuse and exploitation of children,” Leena emphasizes. “Our vision is to create a safe world for children, and we want to do this with a preventive methodology.”Every Donation MattersYour support will help Missing Link Trust continue its vital work of rescuing, rehabilitating, and preventing the trafficking of young girls. The funds raised will go directly towards:Prevention programs in vulnerable areas to stop trafficking before it happens.Rehabilitation services for girls rescued from red-light areas, providing them with medical care, emotional support, and education.Awareness campaigns to reach at-risk communities and ensure girls are protected from traffickers.Together, We Can Make a Difference“Why wait for a girl to get trafficked to save her?” asks Leena. With your help, we can stop this horror before it begins. Your donation will help us create a world where no girl is lost to the nightmare of trafficking.Donate to this fundraiser by Give, India’s largest and most trusted online giving platform, to support Missing Link Trust to save young girls from being trafficked and help rehabilitate those rescued.

campaigns.title: Help us Transform Village Education in Ladakh
campaigns.description: Imagine a village where learning knows no boundaries -where elders, youths and children come together to learn, share knowledge, Inspire one another, and grow as a community.Where children learn not just from books, but from the mountains, streams, farms, folksongs and the community's way of knowing and being. This is what we at village Lab Foundation are building in Ladakh - A living, breathing learning ecosystem, rooted in culture, nature, and collection wisdom.The ProblemOur VisionTo build a thriving Ladakh where Indigenous knowledge and practices are revitalised through education empowering children to carry their heritage forward with Confidence, Pride, and Purpose.﻿Our Programs𝐘𝐨𝐮𝐧𝐠 𝐑𝐞𝐚𝐝𝐞𝐫𝐬 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐦 (𝐘𝐑𝐏)The Young Readers Program focuses on language development and foundational literacy for children grades 2 - 5 through interactive reading and storytelling sessions. It also establishes mini-libraries across villages in Ladakh and works to create a collection of culturally relevant resources for local children.𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐋𝐚𝐝𝐚𝐤𝐡𝐢 𝐖𝐚𝐲 𝐚𝐟𝐭𝐞𝐫-𝐬𝐜𝐡𝐨𝐨𝐥 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐦The Ladakhi Way After-School Program is a culturally rooted initiative designed for students in grades 6 to 9. It combines experiential learning with traditional knowledge, using the village as a learning lab and local elders and youth as facilitators. Through hands-on activities, community engagement, and creative projects, the program fosters critical thinking, communication skills, and a deep connection to Ladakh’s heritage—empowering learners to grow with confidence and purpose.WHAT WILL YOUR CONTRIBUTION EMPOWER?"I once thought I had to leave my village to find purpose. But it was in returning that I discovered it had always been here, in the mountains, the people, and the stories waiting to be held with care.”- Rigzen Wangmo, Founder-Village Lab Foundation LadakhOnce you've made your contribution, don’t stop there, share the love!Pass this campaign along to your friends, family, and kindred hearts. The more we share, the further this dream travels.Your support means the world to us. 💛Stay close to the journey, follow us on:Linkedln: Village Lab Foundation-LadakhInstagram: Village Lab Foundation-LadakhFacebook: Village Lab Foundation, Ladakhfor glimpses of the movement you’re powering.Together, let’s reimagine learning, one village, one child, one act of love at a time #TheLadakhiWay

campaigns.title: Help vulnerable children achieve a bright future
campaigns.description: According to UNICEF, India is home to an estimated 31 million orphans- the largest youth population in the world. Out of these nearly 176 million youth are considered to be in need of care and protection. Shelter Homes mostly are overloaded and focused more on basic needs than youth care or longer-term progression. The result is that both quality of life and adult outcomes for youth in this demographic are very poor, leading to cycles of poverty and abandonment. This is compounded by the fact that little useful data exists because it has historically been difficult to track what happens to children after they leave shelters.  Make A Difference’s vision is that even the most vulnerable children in shelters are able to realize equitable outcomes. We are acutely aware that interventions when done in one sphere, like providing only education or monetary support, often fail to get the desired outcomes. What we do is run multiple interventions to ensure a child's all round development so that they transform into adults who are financially & emotionally independent.We do this via three broad intervention strategies :1. Empower children through better emotional health, life skills, exposure, educational support, transition readiness and after care.2. Improve the ecosystem around them by driving better practices among the institutions that directly affect their lives; primarily shelters, schools, family, state and society.3. Enable the sector through better data, sharing knowledge and building coalitions and networks.It costs us Rs.18,000/- (1500/- per month) per child to implement these programs & ensure they are able to break out of the cycle of poverty.  We run three projects - Ed Support, Propel, and Dream Camp, which are interventions targeted to solve specific problems that the children face in shelter homes.92% of children in shelter homes typically drop out of school by the age of 18. However, we have reversed the trend for those who have been through our programs, and more than 90% of the children we work with have continued into higher education post 18.Percentage of children in our program who passed their 10th board exam - 96%91% of our youth leaving high school have been placed in colleges/jobs/vocational training.Read more about our impact at www.makeadiff.in

campaigns.title: Post Covid-19 Rehabilitation of Youth with Disabilities through Skilling
campaigns.description:
India’s Lockdown to prevent the spread of Covid-19 virus and measures taken to manage the pandemic, had devastating effect on businesses and communities. The crisis continues post lockdown also. But the severity of impact of COVID-19 has been most intense among the people who are most marginalised and vulnerable. Persons with Disabilities (PwDs) are among the hardest hit. Most often they and their families get left out from the mass welfare measures of government agencies or during distribution of life saving provisions done from a central point in their locality. They are also the worst impacted due to the loss of livelihood and are also most likely to fall into further poverty and ill health if they are not able to access the immediate relief for survival and sustainable livelihood.  
 Project Goals and Activities: ·        To support 60 poor and marginalized young people and their families combat the impact of Covid ·        To support them access livelihood opportunities in the domains of Fitter and Electrical and Electronics Mechanics. Among these 80% will be Youth with Disability and 20% without disability. Project Activities: The Foundation/Life Skill Training:  During mobilizing the young people for skill trainings, some YwDs identified require some basic life skill training before being able to decide about taking up a vocation. About 40-50% of the prospective trainees undergo about 20-25 days Foundation course to prepare them on hygiene and soft skills. They are provided with rehabilitation and aids and appliances to make them functionally independent and job ready. Their assessment is done to establish alignment between their physical disabilities, aspirations and demands of the job. The Skill (Domain) Training: Fitter: A six-month integrated course that prepares trainees to work as factory technicians -operate conventional machines, use hand tools and measuring tools and perform machine assembly. Students can also opt for advanced training in Auto CAD and CNC machines after the course for senior positions. Electronics Mechanic: A six-month integrated course in basic Electronic Engineering – circuit design, electronic components, tools and equipment’s, soldering and de-soldering skills, repair and maintenance of consumer and industrial appliances. Modalities of the courses: Duration: 6 months/8 hour classes – 4 hours of theory and 4 hours of practical classes. Courses are run in two batches for each course, i.e., four batches are run simultaneously. 20 students are taken in each batch, out of which 15 students complete the course after accounting in dropouts, resulting in 15*4=60 students completing the course. Placement and post placement support after completion of Skills Training-The youth are placed in industries with a minimum wage of Rs.8,000/- to Rs.10,000 per month. APD ensures >80% placement of the trained candidates and a post placement follow-up support of up-to 3 months to ensure retention. The past trend shows about 70% trainees are retained in their jobs. Subjects taught: Electronics Mechanic :·        Safety of personal working and the equipment·        Familiarization of basic electricity·        Identification of various active & passive components and their applications. ·        Identification and applications of different Hand tools ·        Identification, uses of Measuring Instruments & Electronic equipment ·        Uses of electronic components in different circuits ·        As part of the course they are provided with on the job training (OJT).  Fitter :·        Safety of personal working and the machineries ·        Familiarization of basic fittings and filings ·        Identification and applications of different Hand tools ·        Identification of metals and their applications·        Identification, uses of Measuring Instruments  ·        Internal & External thread cuttings using Tap and die Sets ·        Operations of various machines such as Lathe, Drilling, Grinding ·        Usages of gauges - feeler, screw, pitch, radius, wire gauge  APD’s ELT training programs are co-created with experienced employers and aligned broadly with NCVT & MES (modular employable skill for short term course).This collaboration with industry ensures that our graduates are familiar with current industrial needs & best practices and have the relevant job skills that employers are looking for in entry level graduates. . APD has fixed about 4% i.e., Rs. 2000- as part contribution fees for 6 months residential training. However, the trend so far of trainees paying has been not encouraging as our recruits are from the most indigent and poorest sections of society. APD as a policy does not reject any applications if the trainees fail to pay their contribution, which is more likely in the current Covid-19 situation. 
 Young men and women will get an opportunity to have alternative livelihood post Covid-19 Employers are seeking and promoting placement of technical candidates.Families of youth with disability are sensitized to disability issues, entitlements, economic and social independence 90% of enrolled YWDs state they have gained technical skills Technical Trained youth are competent for employmentEnhanced Functional independence of the candidatesPlacement for 80-90% of the trained candidates Employers are seeking and promoting placement of candidates.PWDs are employed with prerequisite skills.

campaigns.title: Help abandoned elderly with shelter and care
campaigns.description: Every day, elderly parents are left alone on the streets, at train stations, or even outside temples. Many are sick, struggling with age-related problems, and have no money or family to turn to. Some suffer for weeks or months before anyone notices their pain. The heartbreaking truth? They were abandoned by their own families.Although Indian law requires children and relatives to care for senior citizens, many elders still face neglect, abuse, and abandonment. The problem is only getting worse.But there is hope. Our dedicated NGO partners work day and night to rescue these forgotten elders. They provide safe shelter, medical care, nutritious meals, and most importantly—love and respect. Through old age homes across the country, our partners give these seniors a chance to live their remaining years with dignity, comfort, and peace.You can make a difference. Your donation will help our NGO partners:Rescue homeless elders from the streetsProvide shelter, food, and medical careRestore their faith in humanity with kindness and supportNo elder should spend their final years alone and suffering. Your generosity can give them a fresh start. Donate today and help an abandoned senior citizen find safety, care, and hope.
